coredata.dc:title: Co, N-doped carbon dot nanozymes with acid pH-independence and substrate selectivity for biosensing and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Li
Qin, Sainan
Cai, Yexi
Wang, Liang
Dong, Wenpei
Mao, Guojiang
Feng, Suling
Xie, Zhongjian
Zhang, Han
coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes are more stable, economical, and easier to produce than natural enzymes; however, their low activity in non-acidic environments and lack of substrate selectivity severely limit the applications of nanozymes. In this study, we developed Co- and N-doped carbon dot nanozymes (CoNCDs) that specifically exhibited peroxidase-like activity toward o-phenylenediamine in a non-acidic environment. Based on the peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence property of CoNCDs in a neutral environment, a one-pot multi-mode sensing platform (colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence imaging) was developed to detect cholesterol and xanthine in solution and human serum samples. This platform was simple to operate, and the CoNCDs demonstrated a reduced detection time and an improved analytical performance compared to most of the reported nanozymes. Moreover, the improved peroxidase activity at neutral pH, good biocompatibility, and photostability of CoNCDs facilitated their use for monitoring endogenous H2O2 levels in vivo. This study not only provides a novel approach for developing an acid pH-independent and substrate selectivity nanozyme but also demonstrates the application of ratiometric fluorescence imaging using the developed CoNCDs both in vitro and in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co, N-doped carbon dots
Nanozymes
pH-independence response
Substrate selectivity
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assay of H2O2, xanthine and cholesterol
In vitro and in vivo imaging
The acid pH-independent activity of CoNCDs
The mechanism of peroxidase-like activity of CoNCDs
In vitro and in vivo imaging of H2O2

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was ﬁnancially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21405034 , 21976052 ). Key Scientific Research Project of Education Department of Henan Province ( 22A150042 ). National Scientific Research Project Cultivation Funding of Henan Normal University ( 2019PL12 ). Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation ( 2019B1515120043 ). Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (Grant No. 2020A151501612 ). Longhua District Science and Innovation Commission Project Grants of Shenzhen ( JCYJ201904 ), and Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen (Grant No. KCXFZ20201221173413038 ) are grateful acknowledged.

Longhua District Science and Innovation Commission Project Grants of Shenzhen (JCYJ201904), and Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen (Grant No. KCXFZ20201221173413038) are grateful acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the CoNCDs (Inset: size distributions and HRTEM image of the CoNCDs). (b) XRD pattern of CoNCDs. High-resolution XPS spectrum (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) O 1s and (f) Co 2p of CoNCDs.

(a) The UV–vis spectra of CoNCDs and NCDs. (b) The fluorescence spectra of CoNCDs. (c) The UV–vis absorption spectra of different systems. (d) Time-dependent absorbance at 420 nm with different systems. (e) Time-dependent absorbance at 420 nm with different catalysts.

(a) The relative activities (the ratio of the absorbance value in different pH and the max absorbance value) of CoNCDs in different pH values of PBS. (b) The zeta potential values of CoNCDs in different pH solutions. (c) The zeta potentials of CoNCDs during the catalytic oxidation of OPD and H2O2 for different times at pH 7 and 9. (d) The UV–vis absorption spectra of ABTS in the presence of H2O2 by the CoNCDs at pH 7. (e) The UV–vis absorption spectra of ABTS in the presence of H2O2 and CoNCDs in different pH. (f) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB in the presence of H2O2 and CoNCDs in different pH. (g) The photos of different reaction systems. (h) TEM images of CoNCDs after it catalyze the reaction of H2O2 and TMB.

The related linear relationship of (a) the absorbance at 420 nm and (b) the ratio intensity of I560/I430 with the concentration of cholesterol. The related linear relationship of (c) the absorbance at 420 nm and (d) the ratio intensity of I560/I430 with the concentration of xanthine. Inset: the photograph of the colored products and the absorbance and fluorescent spectra in the presence of different concentration of cholesterol and xanthine.

Ratiometric fluorescence imaging. (a) Images of HeLa cells incubated with 1) PBS, 2) CoNCDs and OPD, 3) PMA, CoNCDs and OPD, 4) PMA, NAC, CoNCDs and OPD. (b) Images of mouse injected with 1) PBS, 2) CoNCDs and OPD, 3 and 4) CoNCDs, OPD and H2O2, 20 min and 40 min respectively.
Schematic illustration of the formation process of CoNCDs with acid pH-independence and substrate selectivity.
(a) Colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent multi-mode assay strategy for sensing H2O2. (b) Schematic illustration of one-pot detection method of cholesterol and xanthine based on multi-mode sensor.

head.ce:title: Co, N-doped carbon dot nanozymes with acid pH-independence and substrate selectivity for biosensing and bioimaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozymes are more stable, economical, and easier to produce than natural enzymes; however, their low activity in non-acidic environments and lack of substrate selectivity severely limit the applications of nanozymes. In this study, we developed Co- and N-doped carbon dot nanozymes (CoNCDs) that specifically exhibited peroxidase-like activity toward o-phenylenediamine in a non-acidic environment. Based on the peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence property of CoNCDs in a neutral environment, a one-pot multi-mode sensing platform (colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence imaging) was developed to detect cholesterol and xanthine in solution and human serum samples. This platform was simple to operate, and the CoNCDs demonstrated a reduced detection time and an improved analytical performance compared to most of the reported nanozymes. Moreover, the improved peroxidase activity at neutral pH, good biocompatibility, and photostability of CoNCDs facilitated their use for monitoring endogenous H2O2 levels in vivo. This study not only provides a novel approach for developing an acid pH-independent and substrate selectivity nanozyme but also demonstrates the application of ratiometric fluorescence imaging using the developed CoNCDs both in vitro and in vivo.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes are emerging nanomaterials that exhibit enzyme-like activities. Widely explored in recent years, nanozymes are more stable, economical, and easier to prepare than natural enzymes, which has enabled their use in catalysis, biosensing, bioimaging, and cancer therapy. Recently, numerous nanomaterials with peroxidase-, oxidase-, catalase-, and superoxide dismutase-mimicking activities have been discovered [1–6]. In particular, peroxidase-like nanozymes, such as metals [7], metal oxides [8–10], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [11–14], carbon-based materials [15–20], and single-atom materials, have been widely developed [21–26]. Nevertheless, essential differences exist between peroxidase-like nanozymes and their corresponding natural enzymes (such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP)). For example, most peroxidase-like nanozymes are active under acidic conditions not found in physiological environments; moreover, they lack substrate selectivity.

Several strategies have been proposed to overcome the limitations of peroxidase-like nanozymes. For instance, Niu et al. reported a surface charge engineering strategy to improve the catalytic activity of CuS at neutral pH via aspartic acid modification [27]. Another study, using vitriolization strategy, reported that CoFe2O4 exhibits peroxidase-like activity in neutral or weak alkaline solutions [28]. Hu et al. demonstrated that the catalytic activity of Au nanoclusters at neutral pH can be accelerated by adding heparin [29]. Furthermore, Liu et al. engineered substrate binding pockets via molecular imprinting on the nanozyme surface to enhance substrate specificity [30]. These strategies are complicated, expensive, and time-consuming. Therefore, a facile method is needed to obtain nanozymes that exhibit substrate specificity and favorable activity at neutral pH.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted attention as nanozymes owing to their biocompatibility and catalytic properties [31]. It is reported doping heteroatoms and metal into the CDs could not only increase the number of its catalytically active center but also influences its charge distribution [32]. Inspired by this results, we herein synthesized Co and N-doped carbon dots (CoNCDs) with Co(NO3)2·6H2O and EDTA as raw materials. The obtained CoNCDs specifically exhibited peroxidase-like activity toward substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD) at non-acidic pH ( Scheme 1). Based on these findings, CoNCDs are known to overcome limitations of acidic environment dependence and lack of substrate selectivity of nanozymes, thereby narrowing the gap with natural enzymes. Due to the fluorescent property and peroxidase-like activity of CoNCDs at neutral environment, the fluorescent CoNCDs were further used for multi-mode detection (for easy operation, fast detection, and good analytical performance) of cholesterol and xanthine in one-pot method. In addition, the CoNCDs nanozyme was used for the ratiometric fluorescent monitoring of H2O2 in vitro and in vivo. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to report the utility of nanozymes for multi-mode sensing of analytes by one-pot method and to apply ratiometric fluorescent nanozymes in vivo imaging of H2O2.

Co(NO3)2·6H2O (1.2 M) and ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-Na)(0.6 M) were dissolved in 20 mL double distilled water and 10 mL methanol to prepare the Na2[Co(EDTA)] precursor, a pink precipitate. The pink precipitate was dried in an oven at 60 °C and then calcined in a tube furnace at 300 °C for 2 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 under N2 exposure. The brown calcined product was dissolved in double distilled water, sonicated for 1 h, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was passed through a micropore filter (0.22 µm) to remove non-fluorescent impurities and sequentially dialyzed, using an MD44 (1000 Da MW cut-off) dialysis tubing, for 48 h. Finally, the brown purified solution was lyophilized to obtain the CoNCDs powder.

As reference materials, N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared as follows: 2.2335 g ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-Na) was calcined in a tube furnace at 300 °C for 2 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min-1 under N2 exposure. The supernatant was passed through a micropore filter (0.22 µm) and sequentially dialyzed using an MD44 (1000 Da MW cut-off) dialysis tubing. Finally, the purified solution was lyophilized to obtain the NCDs powder.
Co@NCDs, as the nanohybrids, was prepared by mixing Co(NO3)2·6H2O and NCDs, followed by centrifugation and drying in an oven at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of H2O2, xanthine and cholesterol
ce:section.ce:para: For H2O2 detection, 100 µL variable concentrations of H2O2, 100 µL 10 mM o-phenylenediamine (OPD), and 100 µL CoNCD solution (12.5 μg mL−1) were mixed and incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. Thereafter, 2.7 mL deionized water was added to the resultant solution. Xanthine and cholesterol were detected using the following one-pot method: xanthine oxidase (XOD) (10 µL 2 U mL−1) or cholesterol oxidase (ChOx) (5 µL 40 U mL−1) and 100 µL variable concentrations of xanthine or cholesterol, 100 µL 10 mM OPD, and 100 µL CoNCD solution (12.5 μg mL−1) were mixed and incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. Thereafter, 100 µL variable concentrations of H2O2, 100 µL 10 mM OPD, and 100 µL CoNCD solution (12.5 μg mL−1) were mixed and incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. The absorbance was measured at 420 nm, and all fluorescence spectra were observed at λex = 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo imaging
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were seeded in glass-bottom culture dishes and cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), containing 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS), 100 U mL−1 penicillin, and 100 mg mL−1 streptomycin, at 37 °C under a 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24 h. To monitor endogenous H2O2 levels, the HeLa cells were treated with 0.2 mg mL−1 phorbol 12-myristate 13-acetate (PMA) and N-acetylcysteine (NAC) for 5 min and further incubated with CoNCDs (200 μg mL−1) and OPD (16.5 μM) for 30 min. Thereafter, cells were washed thrice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to remove the remaining CoNCDs and OPD. The fluorescence signals were observed at λem = 430 and 560 nm.

The 4-week-old nude mice were divided into two groups (n = 3). The first group was injected with 150 µL PBS subcutaneously as a control group and photographed. In the second group, pictures were taken 30 min after subcutaneous injection of CoNCDs (4 mg mL−1) and OPD (10 mM). After the photo was taken, H2O2 (5 mM) was injected subcutaneously again. After 20 min and 40 min, the mice were anesthetized with ether and photographed using a small animal optical imaging system.

The CoNCDs exhibited a narrow size distribution with an average diameter of approximately 2 nm ( Fig. 1a). High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) results (inset of Fig. 1a) indicated that the CoNCDs possessed a crystalline structure with an interplanar distance of 0.21 nm, which corresponded with the characteristic lattice spacing of (100) facets of sp2 graphitic materials [33,34]. Moreover, the XRD pattern of CoNCDs depicted a broad peak, ranging from 8° to 20°, owing to their amorphous structure (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, XPS analysis of the CoNCDs revealed four component C 1 peaks at 285.0, 286.3, and 288.6 eV, corresponding to CC/C-C, CN/C-O, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 1c). Three N 1 peaks at 398.6, 399.8, and 401.7 eV were assigned to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N, respectively (Fig. 1d). Finally, two O 1 peaks at 531.8 and 532.7 eV were ascribed to CO and C–OH/C–O–C bonds, respectively (Fig. 1e).

The Co 2p spectra of CoNCDs comprised Co 2p3/2 (781.5 eV) and Co 2p1/2 (874.3 eV) with two distinct satellite peaks at 786.0 and 802.4 eV (Fig. 1f), respectively, indicating the presence of Co (II). These results illustrated the successful doping of Co into the CoNCDs. The FT-IR spectra revealed a broad absorption peak at 3300 cm−1 that corresponded with the O–H stretching vibration, indicating that the surface of CoNCDs contained numerous hydroxyl groups (Fig. S1).

The CoNCDs exhibited broader band absorption and higher absorption intensity in the UV–vis spectra than pure NCDs owing to the existence of Co-N dopants, thereby producing the Co-coordination component of charge-transfer absorption ( Fig. 2a) [35]. Moreover, a broad absorption band was observed in the range of 500–600 nm, which was adapted to the Q band of the Co-coordination component. The fluorescence emission spectra and intensity of the CoNCDs depended on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2b). The highest fluorescence intensity of CoNCDs was observed at 430 nm under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of CoNCDs was calculated to be 4.46%. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of CoNCDs remained unchanged under different temperatures, pH, ion strengths, and continuous irradiation for 60 min, which indicates that the developed CoNCDs have excellent stability (Fig. S2).

The peroxidase-like activity of CoNCDs was assessed via catalytic oxidation of OPD in the presence of H2O2. Fig. 2c showed the CoNCDs could catalyze the oxidation of OPD in the presence of H2O2 because of the absorbance peak of ox-OPD was appeared in 420 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 2d, only the CoNCDs + OPD + H2O2 system displayed increased absorbance compared to the other systems, which further indicated that the CoNCDs possessed intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. However, under similar conditions, the NCDs alone exhibited remarkably low catalytic activity, which was presumably related to Co-doping in the CoNCDs. Thus, Co2+ and NCDs were mixed to produce Co@NCDs, which exhibited peroxidase-like activity under the same conditions, although this activity was lower than that of the CoNCDs (Fig. 2e). These results confirmed that the Co(II) active center was responsible for the peroxidase-like activity of CoNCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The acid pH-independent activity of CoNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Similarly to that of HRP and other nanozymes [9,36], the peroxidase-like activity of CoNCDs was dependent on pH. However, the CoNCDs exhibited high catalytic activity toward OPD under neutral and alkaline conditions and almost no activity under acidic conditions, which is different from that of most existing nanozymes that function in acidic environments [3,15,37–39] ( Fig. 3a). Furthermore, the CoNCDs exhibited higher catalytic activity in neutral and alkaline Tris-HCl and HCl-NaOH buffer solutions than in the same acidic buffer solutions (Fig. S3), which indicated that the peroxidase-like activity of CoNCDs was not affected by buffer type.

As surface charge is known to affect the catalytic abilities of nanozymes [27], we assessed the surface charge state of CoNCDs at different pH values. As illustrated in Fig. 3b, the CoNCDs exhibited a gradually decreasing zeta potential from pH 3–9, which suggested that the surface of CoNCDs was negatively charged in neutral and alkaline environments compared with that in acidic environments. More negatively charged CoNCDs could trigger the positively charged OPD substrate to adsorb onto its surface through electrostatic interactions. To further confirm this hypothesis, the time-dependent zeta potentials of CoNCDs were measured during the entire catalytic process. Indeed, due to the continuous adsorption of positively charged OPD on CoNCDs surface, the negative charge on the surface of CoNCDs was markedly reduced with prolonged reaction time at neutral and alkaline pH (Fig. 3c). These results confirmed that CoNCDs specifically catalyzed the reaction involving the substrate OPD in neutral and alkaline environments.

Subsequently, the adsorption of OPD and CoNCDs was evaluated via affinity interaction tests. Michaelis–Menten curves were obtained by altering the concentrations of H2O2 and OPD under neutral and alkaline conditions (Fig. S4), and kinetic parameters were obtained from the Lineweaver–Burk equation, including the Michaelis-Menten constant (K
m ) and maximum initial velocity (V
max ). As summarized in Table S1, the K
m value of CoNCDs with OPD at pH 9 was 3.6-fold lower than that at pH 7, indicating that CoNCDs possessed a higher affinity for OPD under alkaline conditions. As a near neutral pH is required in biological systems, PBS (pH = 7.4) was selected as the reaction medium for the catalysis reaction of CoNCDs. Furthermore, effects of temperature and H2O2 concentration on the catalytic activity of CoNCDs were also investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S5, the optimal temperature and H2O2 concentration were 37 °C and 0.6 mM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of peroxidase-like activity of CoNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the mechanism underlying the peroxidase-like activity of CoNCDs, double reciprocal plots were constructed for initial velocity against substrate concentration. Fig. S6 illustrates a ping-pong mechanism with parallel lines, indicating that the CoNCDs catalytically decomposed H2O2 to the hydroxyl radical (·OH), which acted as the transition product. Accordingly, we used 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin trap to capture ·OH via electron spin resonance (EPR) spectroscopy. The DMPO/·OH signal was observed in the presence of CoNCDs (Fig. S7), which indicated that the peroxidase-like activity of CoNCDs originated from the decomposition of H2O2 to ·OH.

Specificity is a key parameter for evaluating natural enzymes; however, most nanozymes do not possess a substrate binding pocket, which leads to poor specificity. Interestingly, although the CoNCDs did not have a binding pocket, they exhibited selectivity toward the OPD substrate. In order to confirm the substrate selectivity of CoNCDs, two other typical substrates (2,2′-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonate) [ABTS] and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine [TMB]) were selected as controls. The CoNCDs+ABTS+H2O2 system revealed no absorbance at 417 nm under neutral conditions (Fig. 3d) or between pH 3–9 (Fig. 3e). These results indicated that the CoNCDs exhibited no peroxidase-like activity toward ABTS and no relationship with pH. This is likely due to electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged ABTS and CoNCD molecules. However, the CoNCDs did not exhibit any peroxidase-like activity toward positively charged TMB, between pH 3–9 (Fig. 3f). Moreover, the well-dispersed CoNCDs were aggregated in the presence of TMB and H2O2 (Fig. 3g,h), which likely reduced the exposure of CoNCD active sites and affected their catalytic performance. These results indicated the substrate-selective peroxidase-like activity of CoNCDs toward OPD.

To further confirm the intrinsic substrate selectivity of CoNCDs, we individually assessed the catalytic efficiency of other Co-based peroxidase-like nanozymes (NiCo2O4 and Co3O4) to oxidize OPD, ABTS, and TMB in the presence of H2O2. Compared to CoNCDs, the Co3O4 and NiCo2O4 nanozymes did not exhibit selective catalytic oxidation for any of the three substrates (Fig. S8). Therefore, the obtained substrate selectivity was presumably related to the intrinsic structure and composition of CoNCDs.

Colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence multi-mode sensing of H2O2 was developed in neutral media based on the peroxidase-like activity and fluorescent property of CoNCDs. The sensing platform for H2O2 is depicted in Scheme 2a, wherein CoNCDs catalyzed the oxidation of OPD in the presence of H2O2 to form a yellow product (ox-OPD) with an absorption peak of 420 nm, which then quenched the fluorescence of CoNCDs and generated a new fluorescence emission peak at 560 nm. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CoNCDs is presumably due to the inner filter effect (IFE). As illustrated in Fig. S9, the excitation spectra of CoNCDs overlapped with the absorption spectrum of ox-OPD, and H2O2 and OPD did not individually affect the fluorescence of CoNCDs (Fig. S9).

Thus, the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method for determining H2O2 was developed based on the absorbance of ox-OPD at 420 nm and the fluorescence intensity ratio of ox-OPD to CoNCDs (I560/I430) (Fig. S10). A linear relationship was obtained between the absorbance and the H2O2 concentration ranging from 0.33 to 233 μM (A420 = 0.0011 C + 0.0076, R2 = 0.995). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I560/I430) was linearly related to H2O2 concentration in the range of 0.33–233 μM (I560/I430 = 0.0014 C + 0.074, R2 = 0.997). The detection limit (DL) of the colorimetric and fluorescent sensor for H2O2 were 0.15 and 0.13 μM.

Since the CoNCDs exhibited peroxidase-like activity under neutral conditions, a one-pot colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method was developed to detect cholesterol and xanthine (Scheme 2b). Species such as cholesterol, ChOx, CoNCDs, and OPD (or xanthine, XOD, CoNCDs, and OPD) were synchronously incubated in a neutral buffer for 40 min. During the incubation process, ChOx (or XOD) catalyzed the oxidation of cholesterol (or xanthine) to generate H2O2 in the presence of dissolved O2. The generated H2O2 was quantitatively detected, with the absorbance at 420 nm increasing with elevated cholesterol concentrations to yield a linear equation for the colorimetric method in the range of 0.33–33.3 μM (A420 = 0.0071 C+0.069, R2 = 0.998) ( Fig. 4a). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity decreased at 430 nm and increased at 560 nm with an increase in cholesterol concentration (Fig. 4b). The fluorescence intensity ratio (I560/I430) was linearly related to cholesterol concentration in the range of 0.33–33.3 μM (I560/I430 = 0.0053 C+ 0.11, R2 = 0.999). The DL of the colorimetric and fluorescence sensor for cholesterol was 0.14 μM. Similarly, the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method was used to detect xanthine (Fig. 4c,d), yielding a linear response in the range of 0.45–16.7 μM, with a DL of 0.14 μM. As summarized in Table S2, the developed CoNCDs not only provided a sensing platform with easy operation and fast detection but also exhibited comparable or even superior sensitivity to other nanozymes used for cholesterol and xanthine detection. Most importantly, the CoNCDs possessed multiple fluorescence signal outputs, which exhibited good anti-interference, offered more accuracy results and selected the detection method according to the practice situation, than the corresponding nanozymes with single signal outputs.

Furthermore, only cholesterol and xanthine altered the colorimetric and fluorescence signals of the sensor even if the interferent concentration was approximately 5-fold higher than that of cholesterol and xanthine, which might be related to the catalytic specificity of ChOx and XOD (Figs. S11 and S12). Additionally, the cholesterol levels of different human serum samples were analyzed using the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence platform (Table S3). The recoveries ranged from 95.2% to 104.2%, indicating that cholesterol was successfully detected in human serum samples. Moreover, similar recovery results for xanthine in human serum samples demonstrated the applicability of the sensing platform to monitor xanthine in biological samples (Table S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo imaging of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Toxicity of CoNCDs and OPD towards HeLa cells was evaluated via the MTT assay. Cell viability was > 85% for 0–250 μg·mL−1 CoNCDs and > 75% for 0–30 μM OPD (Fig. S13), indicating the high biocompatibility of the sensor. Moreover, we explored the potential of CoNCDs and the OPD system for sensing endogenous H2O2. Phorbol-12-myristate-13-acetate (PMA) was used to induce inflammation in HeLa cells to produce H2O2, and N-acetylcysteine (NAC) was used as a scavenger to eliminate H2O2. After incubating HeLa cells with CoNCDs and OPD at 37 °C for 30 min, confocal microscopy revealed strong fluorescence in the blue channel and almost no fluorescence in the green channel ( Fig. 5a). Furthermore, fluorescence in the green channel increased along with quenched fluorescence in the blue channel when cells were treated with PMA and then incubated with CoNCDs and OPD for 30 min. Additionally, fluorescence was observed in the blue channel but disappeared in the green channel when control cells were pretreated with PMA after sequential treatment with NAC and eventually incubated with CoNCDs and OPD. These results suggest that the CoNCDs could be promising agents for in vitro imaging of endogenous H2O2.

The potential of CoNCDs for in vivo imaging of H2O2 was also assessed. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, control mice exhibited no fluorescence in both channels, but mice treated with CoNCDs and OPD exhibited a strong fluorescence in the blue channel and a weak fluorescence in the green channel. Comparatively, the fluorescence of mice treated with CoNCDs, OPD, and H2O2 was gradually weakened in the blue channel and gradually became stronger in the green channel, supporting the view that CoNCDs can be used to monitor H2O2 levels in vivo.

In summary, the CoNCDs developed in the current study were prepared using a simple method and demonstrated peroxidase-like activity in non-acidic environments. Additionally, the CoNCDs displayed substrate-selective peroxidase-like activity toward OPD. Further, this study developed a one-pot colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence multi-mode sensor using CoNCDs and OPD to detect cholesterol and xanthine in solution and serum samples. More importantly, the CoNCDs were applied as ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes to monitor the changes in H2O2 levels in vitro and in vivo. This study not only provides a novel approach for developing nanozymes with peroxidase-like activity under neutral conditions and substrate selectivity but also highlights the application of nanozymes in live imaging of H2O2, which will help further our understanding of the role of H2O2 in biological systems, especially diseases.

Li Su: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Sainan Qin: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Yexi Cai: Methodology, Investigation. Liang Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Wenpei Dong: Methodology. Guojiang Mao: Methodology. Suling Feng: Methodology. Zhongjian Xie: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Han Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Longhua District Science and Innovation Commission Project Grants of Shenzhen (JCYJ201904), and Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen (Grant No. KCXFZ20201221173413038) are grateful acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.131150.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Su is currently an associate professor at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, China. Her research interests focus on the design and application of nanozymes.

Sainan Qin received her master’s degree at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, China. Her research interests focus on the synthesis and application of colorimetric and fluorescent sensor based on nanozymes.

Yexi Cai received her master’s degree at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, China. Her research interests focus on the synthesis and application of colorimetric and fluorescent sensor based on nanozymes.

Liang Wang is currently an associate professor at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, China. His research interests focus on the synthesis and application of nanomaterials.

Wenpei Dong is currently an experimentalist at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, China. Her research interests focus on the biological effect of nanomaterials and biological activity of drugs.

Guojiang Mao is currently an associate professor at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, China. His research interests focus on the design and application of fluorescent probes based on fluorescent dyes.

Suling Feng is currently a professor at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Her research interests focus on the design and application of fluorescent nanomaterials.
Zhongjian Xie is currently a professor in Institute of Pediatrics, Shenzhen Children’s Hospital. His research interests are biosensing and bioimaging, biomedical science.
Han Zhang is currently a professor in Shenzhen University. His research interests are biosensing and bioimaging, fiber laser, nonlinear optics, fiber communication and electronic devices.
